plan_metadata.name: Sample One Productivity Hub using Toolkit Remote Debug

steps.description: Enter the text 'One Productivity Hub using Toolkit' into the search input box located at the top of the 'Samples' pane in the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface.
Click the "Create" button for the "One Productivity Hub using Graph..." sample in the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface.
Press the "Enter" key to confirm the selection of the default folder path in the "Workspace Folder" dialog box within the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface in Visual Studio Code.
@assertion 'Preview README.MD' is visible
@assertion the page title contains 'Getting Started with One Productivity Hub'
Perform a right-click on the tab titled "Preview README.md" located in the upper interface of Visual Studio Code within the active workspace.
Click the "Close" option in the context menu of the file tab within Visual Studio Code to close the currently active file (Preview README.md).
Press the F1 key to open the Command Palette in Visual Studio Code on the main interface screen.
Type 'View: Show Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit' into the command palette search bar in Visual Studio Code.
Press the 'Enter' key to confirm the selection "View: Show Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit" in the Visual Studio Code command palette.
Press the F1 key to open the Command Palette in Visual Studio Code, specifically within the context of the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit extension interface.
Click the "View: Show Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit" option in the Command Palette in Visual Studio Code.
Start to Login M365 Account. Type text 'Microsoft 365 Agents: Accounts' into the Visual Studio Code Command Palette input field labeled 'Type the name of a command to run'.
Click the "Microsoft 365 Agents: Accounts" option in the command palette to select it.
Click on the "Sign in to Microsoft 365" option in the dropdown menu within the Visual Studio Code interface at coordinates (304, 54).
Click the "Sign in" button in the "Create a Microsoft 365 developer sandbox" dialog at the top of the Visual Studio Code application interface.
Type the text 'test14@xxbdw.onmicrosoft.com' into the 'Email or phone' input field on the Microsoft login page.
Press "Enter" to confirm email input and proceed by simulating a click on the "Next" button within the Microsoft Sign-in prompt.
Type ${{secret:M365_ACCOUNT_PASSWORD}} into the password field on the Microsoft login page, under the 'Enter password' prompt.
Press the "Enter" key after entering a password on the Microsoft login screen.
Click the "Close" button (red cross) in the browser tab hosting the Visual Studio Code sign-in confirmation page.
@assertion there's 'test14@xxbdw.onmicrosoft.com' in the "ACCOUNTS" section
Start to login Azure Account. Press the F1 key to open the command palette in Visual Studio Code.
Type the text 'Microsoft 365 Agents: Accounts' into the Command Palette input field located at the top of the Visual Studio Code interface.
Click the "Login Azure" option in the command palette dropdown, highlighted with the blue background, to select the recently used command.
Click on the "Sign in to Azure" option displayed under "ACCOUNTS" in the Microsoft 365 Agents sidebar within the Visual Studio Code interface.
Click the "Sign in" button in the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit Azure authentication prompt at the top of the Visual Studio Code interface.
Click the "Allow" button in the pop-up dialog prompting to sign in using Microsoft for the "Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit" extension in Visual Studio Code.
Click the "Use another account" option under the "Pick an account" section in the Microsoft sign-in interface.
Enter ${{env:AZURE_ACCOUNT_NAME}} into the 'Email, phone, or Skype' input field on the Microsoft Sign-In page.
Press the "Enter" key to submit the email address in the Microsoft Sign In form displayed on the login.microsoftonline.com page.
Type text ${{secret:AZURE_ACCOUNT_PASSWORD}} into the 'Password' field on the Microsoft login page (URL: login.microsoftonline.com).
Press the "Enter" key to submit the password and proceed with the sign-in on the Microsoft login page.
Click the browser's "Close" button (red 'X') in the top-right corner to exit the confirmation page after signing in.
@assertion there's ${{env:AZURE_ACCOUNT_NAME}} in the "ACCOUNTS" section
Press the F1 key to open the command palette in Visual Studio Code while viewing the Explorer panel.
Type text 'Debug: Select and Start Debugging' into the Visual Studio Code Command Palette (accessible via Ctrl+Shift+P or the 'View' menu).
Press the "Enter" key to select the highlighted command "Debug: Select and Start Debugging" within the Visual Studio Code Command Palette.
Type 'Launch Remote (Chrome)' into the Command Palette search bar in Visual Studio Code to select the corresponding debug option.
Press "Enter" to select the "Launch Remote (Chrome)" option in the debugging configuration dropdown within Visual Studio Code.
Click on the "Email, phone, or Skype" input field in the Microsoft Sign-In page.
Type the email address 'test14@xxbdw.onmicrosoft.com' into the 'Email or phone' input field on the Microsoft Sign-In page.
Press the "Enter" key to submit the entered email address in the Microsoft "Sign in" interface on the login.microsoftonline.com webpage.
Type text: ${{secret:M365_ACCOUNT_PASSWORD}} into the 'Password' field on the Microsoft login page.
Press the "Enter" key to submit the password on the Microsoft login page interface.
Click the "Yes" button on the "Stay signed in?" dialog of the Microsoft login page.
Click the "Add" button to install the "graph-connector-app" in Microsoft Teams.
Click the "Open" button in the "Added successfully!" dialog box within the Microsoft Teams interface.
Click the "Start One Productivity Hub" button located in the center of the application welcome screen within Microsoft Teams.
Click the "Accept" button to grant permissions requested for the application "one-productivity-hub-aad" on the Microsoft login consent page.
@assertion 'Calendar events', 'To-do tasks', 'Files' are visible
